--- Page 1 ---
Cloud-OpsBench: A Reproducible Benchmark for Agentic
Root Cause Analysis in Cloud Systems
YILUN WANG, The Chinese University of HongKong, China
GUANGBA YU∗, The Chinese University of HongKong, China
HAIYU HUANG, The Chinese University of HongKong, China
ZIRUI WANG, Sun Yat-sen University, China
YUJIE HUANG, The Chinese University of HongKong, China
PENGFEI CHEN, Sun Yat-sen University, China
MICHAEL R. LYU, The Chinese University of HongKong, China
The transition to agentic Root Cause Analysis (RCA) necessitates benchmarks that evaluate active reasoning
rather than passive classification. However, current frameworks fail to reconcile ecological validity with
reproducibility. We introduce Cloud-OpsBench, a large-scale benchmark that employs a State Snapshot
Paradigm to construct a deterministic digital twin of the cloud, featuring 452 distinct fault cases across
40 root cause types spanning the full Kubernetes stack. Crucially, Cloud-OpsBench serves as an enabling
infrastructure for next-generation SRE research: (1) As a Data Engine, it harvests high-quality reasoning
trajectories to bootstrap Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) for Small Language Models; (2) As an Reinforcement
Learning (RL) environment, it transforms high-risk operations into a safe low-latency sandbox for training
policy optimization agents; and (3) As a Diagnostic Standard, its process-centric protocol uncovers architectural
bottlenecks guiding the design of robust specialized multi-agent system for RCA.
Additional Key Words and Phrases: Large Language Models, Cloud Computing, Root Cause Analysis, Bench-
mark, Agentic AI
ACM Reference Format:
Yilun Wang, Guangba Yu, Haiyu Huang, Zirui Wang, Yujie Huang, Pengfei Chen, and Michael R. Lyu. 2026.
Cloud-OpsBench: A Reproducible Benchmark for Agentic Root Cause Analysis in Cloud Systems. In Proceedings
of Make sure to enter the correct conference title from your rights confirmation email (Conference acronym ’XX).
ACM, New York, NY, USA, 22 pages. https://doi.org/XXXXXXX.XXXXXXX
1
Introduction
The reliability of cloud-native systems constitutes the foundation of modern online services [54,
62]. However, system complexity makes Root Cause Analysis (RCA) notoriously difficult [6, 27].
∗Guangba Yu is the corresponding author.

Kong SAR, China.

prior specific permission and/or a fee. Request permissions from permissions@acm.org.
Conference acronym ’XX, Woodstock, NY
© 2026 Copyright held by the owner/author(s). Publication rights licensed to ACM.
ACM ISBN 978-1-4503-XXXX-X/2018/06

, Vol. 1, No. 1, Article . Publication date: March 2026.
arXiv:2603.00468v1  [cs.SE]  28 Feb 2026


--- Page 2 ---
2
Yilun Wang, Guangba Yu et al.
Traditional discriminative Artificial Intelligence for IT Operations (AIOps) models (e.g., VAEs)
detect anomalies but lack the semantic understanding to explain failures [15, 26, 48, 56]. Recently,
Large Language Models (LLMs) have triggered a paradigm shift towards Agentic RCA [8, 45]. Unlike
passive classifiers, these agents function as autonomous SREs, actively interacting with systems to
verify hypotheses. This transition necessitates benchmarks that evaluate active diagnostic reasoning
rather than static pattern recognition.
However, existing frameworks fail to balance ecological validity and reproducibility. (1) Static
Telemetry Benchmarks (e.g., LogHub [65], RCAEval [36]) rely on retrospective data dumps. They
reduce agents to passive readers, ignoring the essential capability of interactive tool use. (2) Dynamic
Environment Benchmarks (e.g., AIOpsLab [7]) deploy live microservices but suffer from stochasticity.
Non-deterministic factors (e.g., network jitter) introduce noise, rendering rigorous A/B testing
impossible. Furthermore, reliance on outcome-based metrics (e.g., Accuracy@1) rewards spurious
guesses, failing to distinguish systematic reasoning from statistical probability.
To bridge this gap, we present Cloud-OpsBench, the first large-scale reproducible benchmark
on Kubernetes for Agentic RCA. At its core is the State Snapshot Paradigm, which functions as
a deterministic digital twin. Instead of relying on transient live clusters [7, 43], Cloud-OpsBench
freezes the complete operational context (control plane objects, metrics, logs) into an immutable
persistence layer. Interaction is reconstructed via a mocked interface simulating standard tools (e.g.,
kubectl) with zero latency. This design guarantees 100% reproducibility while supporting complex
tool usage. Cloud-OpsBench comprises 452 distinct fault cases across 40 fault types, covering the
full Kubernetes stack.
Crucially, we position Cloud-OpsBench not merely as a leaderboard, but as an enabling infras-
tructure for next-generation SRE research. Our empirical evaluation serves to validate its utility in
three key dimensions: (1) As a Data Engine, it demonstrates that procedural demonstrations out-
perform declarative knowledge, proving its value in harvesting trajectories to bootstrap Supervised
Fine-Tuning (SFT); (2) As a Diagnostic Standard, its process-centric protocol uncovers architectural
bottlenecks (e.g., “Redundancy Paradox” in DeepSeek-V3.2 and “Syntactic Fragility” in Qwen-14B)
guiding the design of specialized multi-agent swarms; (3) As an RL Environment, its deterministic
nature provides the necessary stability for training future agents via policy optimization.
In summary, this paper makes the following contributions:
• A Process-Centric Evaluation Paradigm. We propose Cloud-OpsBench, the first white-box
benchmark for Agentic RCA. By quantifying trajectory alignment, we demonstrate that how an
agent verifies is as critical as what it concludes, establishing a new standard for reliability.
• An Enabling Data Engine for Model Bootstrapping. We establish Cloud-OpsBench as a
high-quality data engine. By validating that 14B-parameter models using our procedural demon-
strations can rival proprietary giants, we provide a cost-efficient blueprint for bootstrapping
private SRE agents via SFT.
• A Safe Sandbox for Policy Optimization. We construct a high-fidelity Digital Twin that
transforms AIOps from a risky operational task into a safe low-latency learning environment.
This unlocks the capability to train agents via RL to dynamically optimize exploration strategies.
2
Motivation and Challenges
2.1
The Paradigm Shift: From Statistical Detection to Agentic Root Cause Analysis
The field of AIOps is currently undergoing a fundamental transition from discriminative artificial
intelligence to generative agents. This shift represents a change in both the definition of failure and
the mechanism of diagnosis. In the era dominated by deep learning, failures were primarily defined
as statistical deviations [29, 51–53]. The objective of traditional AIOps models (e.g., VAEs [48],
, Vol. 1, No. 1, Article . Publication date: March 2026.


--- Page 3 ---
Cloud-OpsBench: A Reproducible Benchmark for Agentic Root Cause Analysis in Cloud Systems
3

Fig. 1. Comparison of benchmarking paradigms.
LSTMs [26]) was to model the normal distribution of system metrics. In this context, RCA were
treated as curve-fitting or classification tasks [15, 56], where the goal was to identify data points
that diverged significantly from historical baselines. The emergence of LLMs has redefined the
problem [1, 8, 28]. The focus has moved toward identifying the logical conflicts between observed
states and expected behaviors defined in system protocols [33, 50].
Consequently, the role of the AI model has evolved from passive classifiers to autonomous SREs.
Unlike their predecessors, these agentic SREs must possess three critical operational competencies:
• Active Perception: The ability to proactively query system states via tools rather than passively
ingesting dumps.
• Knowledge Grounding: The capacity to synthesize telemetry with external documentation.
• Deductive Reasoning: The capability to verify hypotheses through multi-step logic.
However, existing benchmarks [2, 3, 10, 31, 36, 41, 49, 53, 57, 65] largely remain aligned with the
previous paradigm of statistical pattern recognition (details shown in Table 1). This discrepancy
creates a significant threat to construct validity. As detailed in the following sections, current
benchmarks fail to adequately measure the specific capabilities required for Agentic RCA across
three critical dimensions: data modality, environmental interaction, and evaluation metrics.
2.2
Gap 1: The Ecological Validity Gap in Static Telemetry Artifacts
To function as an SRE, an agent must interact with the system. However, most existing benchmarks,
such as TimeSeriesBench [41], LogHub [65], RCAEval [36], and LogEval [10], are fundamentally
constructed upon static telemetry artifacts, as illustrated in Fig. 1(a). These artifacts are retrospective
collections of logs, metrics, or traces that have been dumped and stored after an incident has
occurred. These retrospective collections of logs or metrics effectively strip away the environment,
reducing the agent from an engineer to a reader. This static paradigm fails to motivate or measure
the core competencies of Agentic RCA in three ways:
, Vol. 1, No. 1, Article . Publication date: March 2026.


--- Page 4 ---
4
Yilun Wang, Guangba Yu et al.
1. Interaction Constraints. Real-world SREs rely on defined APIs (e.g., kubectl) to actively
investigate issues. In contrast, benchmarks like LogHub or RCAEval provide only raw data files
without execution interfaces. This absence forces the model to process entire datasets essentially
as a reading comprehension task. This setup implicitly assumes the agent is a passive entity with
infinite context, which contradicts the reality of large-scale operations where telemetry exceeds
gigabytes per hour. By denying the agent the ability to retrieve information, existing benchmarks
fail to evaluate the critical skill of information seeking efficiency.
2. Modality Mismatch. Human experts rarely parse raw high-dimensional tensors; they rely
on monitoring tools to abstract signals into semantic events (e.g., “Latency spike detected”) [55].
Existing benchmarks, however, force a bottom-up, perception-heavy approach, requiring agents
to parse thousands of metric points. This conflates signal processing with diagnostic reasoning.
A motivated agentic benchmark should assess whether an agent can interpret high-level system
states, not whether it can perform arithmetic on CSV rows.
3. Knowledge Vacuum. Benchmarks based on toy systems (e.g., Train Ticket [63]) typically
provide only runtime data, creating a knowledge vacuum. This setup ignores the fact that human
operators rely heavily on external knowledge sources, including troubleshooting guides, API
documentation, and system topology diagrams. Without access to system specifications or known
failure patterns, agents are forced to rely on parametric knowledge or speculation rather than
grounded logical reasoning.
2.3
Gap 2: The Reproducibility Challenge in Dynamic Environments
Recognizing the limitations of static artifacts, recent research has pivoted toward dynamic eval-
uation frameworks [7, 18, 43] that deploy actual microservices and inject faults in real time. As
shown in Fig. 1(b), while this approach improves fidelity, it introduces a critical trade-off regarding
reproducibility and efficiency.
The primary obstacle is the inherent non-determinism of live cloud systems. In a cloud system,
the manifestation of a fault is heavily influenced by non-deterministic factors. For instance, a
network delay injection of 500 milliseconds may trigger a cascade of timeouts in one experimental
run but result in a seamless retry in another. This environmental volatility undermines the fairness
of comparative evaluations. In a scientific benchmark, the test conditions must be invariant across
different models. If Model A fails due to a transient network jitter while Model B succeeds because
the network was stable, the comparison is invalid. Current dynamic frameworks fail to decouple
intrinsic system complexity from experimental noise, rendering rigorous A/B testing impossible.
Beyond stochasticity, the operational overhead of dynamic environments creates a prohibitive
barrier to entry. Maintaining a high-fidelity cluster requires substantial cloud resources, but more
critically, it imposes excessive verification latency. A single evaluation cycle comprising system
deployment, fault injection, agent execution, and environment restoration is inherently slow.
Running a comprehensive benchmark on live systems can consume dozens of machine hours per
iteration [58]. This prolonged feedback loop drastically hinders the iterative development of agent
strategies, making essential tasks like prompt refinement and hyperparameter tuning practically
infeasible for researchers without industrial-scale infrastructure.
2.4
Gap 3: The Evaluation Gap in Outcome-Oriented Metrics
Even if data and environmental challenges are resolved, the prevailing metrics used in AIOps
benchmarks remain a bottleneck. The majority of existing studies continue to rely on standard
classification metrics, such as Precision, Recall, and F1-score [36, 43, 65]. While these metrics are
effective for discriminative models where the goal is a single label prediction, they exhibit a severe
, Vol. 1, No. 1, Article . Publication date: March 2026.


--- Page 5 ---
Cloud-OpsBench: A Reproducible Benchmark for Agentic Root Cause Analysis in Cloud Systems
5
outcome bias when applied to generative agents. This bias obscures the critical distinction between
genuine reasoning and spurious guessing.
The core limitation is that outcome-oriented metrics fail to scrutinize the intermediate reasoning
process [20, 46]. LLMs are known to be susceptible to hallucinations and can exploit shortcuts
in the data distribution. It is possible for an agent to identify the correct root cause based on a
completely erroneous logical path, a phenomenon often described as being right for the wrong
reasons. For instance, an agent might correctly flag a database service as the culprit not because it
retrieved and analyzed the relevant latency metrics, but simply because the database component
appeared frequently in the historical training data associated with generic error keywords.
A benchmark that only evaluates the final output assigns a perfect score to such unreliable behav-
ior. This validates a diagnostic process that lacks grounding in evidence. In a real-time diagnostic
task, the value of an agent lies not just in its conclusion, but in the efficiency and logical coherence
of its investigation. Current metrics are blind to the quality of the investigation. They treat a “logical
but failed” diagnosis equivalent to a “hallucinated and failed” one. Consequently, a comprehensive
evaluation must shift focus from calculating simple prediction accuracy to quantifying reasoning
alignment and efficiency, ensuring that the agent follows a logical evidence-based trajectory similar
to that of a human expert [13, 46].
2.5
Bridging the Gaps: Requirements for Benchmarking Agentic RCA
The transition to Agentic RCA has exposed a dilemma: researchers are currently forced to choose
between static artifacts (reproducible but unrealistic) and live environments (realistic but unstable).
To resolve this and align benchmarking with the true motivation of enabling the Agentic RCA, we
identify three design requirements:
• High-Fidelity Interaction with Deterministic Replay: The environment must support essen-
tial SRE tool-use capabilities (e.g., kubectl) to enable active information seeking, while simultane-
ously guaranteeing deterministic state transitions. This necessitates a shift from live execution to
state snapshotting, where the system state is frozen and served via mock interfaces to ensure
zero-cost and 100% reproducible evaluations.
• Knowledge-Augmented Context: To bridge the epistemic void, the benchmark must integrate
system and operational knowledge (e.g., system documents, troubleshooting guides) alongside
telemetry. The objective is not merely to evaluate retrieval mechanics, but to assess the agent’s
capacity for knowledge-grounded reasoning. Specifically, how well it synthesizes external infor-
mation to diagnose novel issues in an open-book setting.
• Process-Oriented Evaluation: Assessment protocols must move beyond binary success/failure
labels. A rigorous benchmark should quantify the alignment of the diagnosis trajectory, penalizing
agents that hallucinate evidence or execute illogical query sequences, even if they accidentally
arrive at the correct root cause.
3
Cloud-OpsBench
To address the limitations of existing benchmarks, we present Cloud-OpsBench. As illustrated in
Fig. 2, Cloud-OpsBench is designed as a high-fidelity, deterministic, and process-oriented bench-
mark. It comprises 452 distinct fault cases spanning 40 specific cloud fault types, covering the full
Kubernetes stack. The core innovation of Cloud-OpsBench lies in its architectural alignment with
the requirements identified in Section 2.5:
• Addressing Gap 1: We utilize a high-fidelity Kubernetes testbed (§ 3.3.1) and a knowledge-rich
fault generation pipeline (§ 3.3.2) to ensure agents operate in a realistic open-book context.
• Addressing Gap 2: We introduce the State Snapshot paradigm (§ 3.2), which freezes the crime
scene into a deterministic Digital Twin, eliminating noise while preserving interactivity.
, Vol. 1, No. 1, Article . Publication date: March 2026.


--- Page 6 ---
6
Yilun Wang, Guangba Yu et al.

Fig. 2. Overview of Cloud-OpsBench.
• Addressing Gap 3: We formalize the task as a trajectory-based decision process (§ 3.1) and
synthesize canonical reference trajectories, enabling the assessment of reasoning logic rather
than just final outcomes.
3.1
Agentic RCA Task Formulation
To rigorously evaluate both the outcome accuracy and the process validity of SRE agents, we
formalize the Agentic RCA task as a trajectory-based decision process. We define the ground truth
as a tuple R∗= ⟨𝑆,𝐶, 𝑅⟩(denoting Stage, Component, and Root Cause) and a set of expert reference
trajectories {T ∗}. Formally, the agentic diagnosis is modeled as a mapping function 𝑓that produces
a composite output:
𝑓: ⟨𝐴𝑎𝑙𝑒𝑟𝑡, E𝑠𝑛𝑎𝑝𝑠ℎ𝑜𝑡⟩→⟨T, ˆR⟩.
(1)
This process consists of three distinct phases:
1. Input Space: The agent receives a natural language alert 𝐴𝑎𝑙𝑒𝑟𝑡(e.g., “Service unreachable”)and
access to a cached system snapshot E𝑠𝑛𝑎𝑝𝑠ℎ𝑜𝑡. Crucially, E𝑠𝑛𝑎𝑝𝑠ℎ𝑜𝑡serves as a deterministic high-
fidelity cache of the system state, enabling the agent to execute interactive queries (e.g., kubectl
logs) without stochastic noise.
2. Diagnostic Trajectory (T): The agent interacts with the environment to generate a reasoning
trajectory T = [(𝑡1,𝑎1,𝑜1), . . . , (𝑡𝑛,𝑎𝑛,𝑜𝑛)]. At each step 𝑖:
• Thought (𝑡𝑖): The agent analyzes the current context to formulate a hypothesis.
• Action (𝑎𝑖): The agent issues a tool call to probe E𝑠𝑛𝑎𝑝𝑠ℎ𝑜𝑡.
• Observation (𝑜𝑖): The environment returns the pre-recorded execution result.
This trajectory T is treated as part of the system output to enable process-oriented evaluation.
3. Output Space: Upon concluding the investigation, the agent yields a final result consisting of:
• Trajectory (T): The complete sequence of investigative actions, which is evaluated against
expert trajectories {T ∗} for procedural alignment.
• Predicted Diagnosis ( ˆR): The final structured tuple identifying the root cause, which is
evaluated against the ground truth R∗for outcome accuracy.
3.2
Design Philosophy: The State Snapshot Paradigm
The core design philosophy of Cloud-OpsBench addresses the fundamental tension between re-
producibility and ecological validity (§ 2). To bridge this gap, we introduce the State Snapshot
paradigm (depicted in Fig. 1(c)), which conceptually serves as a deterministic digital twin of the
target system at the precise moment of failure. Unlike traditional static artifacts (Gap 1)(§ 2), a State
Snapshot captures the holistic operational context. This encompasses not only historical telemetry
(e.g., logs, metrics) but also the static control plane configurations (e.g., Kubernetes manifests)
and the instantaneous data plane states (e.g., Kubernetes pod lists), thereby freezing the complete
environmental context required for diagnosis.
The fundamental innovation of this paradigm lies in the decoupling of state storage from tool
interaction. In dynamic benchmarks, system state is transient and tightly coupled to a runtime envi-
ronment. In contrast, our approach freezes this state into immutable persistence layers, eliminating
, Vol. 1, No. 1, Article . Publication date: March 2026.


--- Page 7 ---
Cloud-OpsBench: A Reproducible Benchmark for Agentic Root Cause Analysis in Cloud Systems
7
environmental noise. Interactivity is then reconstructed via a mocked operational interface that
acts as a deterministic translation layer. When an agent executes a command like kubectl get
pods, it does not query a volatile API server but rather retrieves data from the frozen snapshot.
This architecture enables standard tool-use within a strictly deterministic environment. By simu-
lating the behavior of native CLI tools on top of static data, Cloud-OpsBench ensures high-fidelity
interaction where agents operate exactly as human SREs would, while guaranteeing reproducibility.
Every execution yields the exact same output regardless of external factors, allowing for rigorous
repetitive evaluation of agentic reasoning trajectories.
3.3
Benchmark Construction
As illustrated in Figure 2, we design a pipeline that bridges unstructured operational knowledge
with deterministic runtime environments. This pipeline integrates a human-verified knowledge
base with a Multi-Agent System (MAS) to generate the final dataset of 452 distinct fault cases.
3.3.1
Benchmark Testbed. Before detailing the generation pipeline, we first introduce the target
cloud-native environment. To ensure ecological validity, we establish a high-fidelity testbed on
Huawei Cloud ECS. The testbed operates on a Kubernetes cluster (v1.31) hosted on 4 instances. As
the foundation of cloud-native infrastructure, Kubernetes offers not only a complex distributed
control plane for fault injection but also a standardized corpus of documentation. This allows us
to rigorously test knowledge-grounded reasoning, distinguishing our approach from benchmarks
based on proprietary or obscure systems. Integrated within this infrastructure is a lightweight native
observability plane designed to support the State Snapshot mechanism. Specifically, we deploy
Prometheus [37] and Istio [17] to capture metrics (e.g., CPU Usage) which power the anomaly
detection tools. Furthermore, we leverage the native Kubernetes API to directly expose real-time
object states and retrieve on-demand container logs, ensuring low-latency access for resource
inspection and log analysis.
To stimulate this infrastructure, we deploy Google Online Boutique [12], the de facto standard
benchmark for cloud-native research. We select this workload not for its specific business logic,
but for its structural representativeness as a cloud-native systems proxy. It comprises 11 polyglot
microservices communicating via gRPC, covering a wide spectrum of generic operational patterns
(e.g., synchronous RPC, asynchronous queuing, and caching). By employing a load generator
(Locust[32]) to simulate continuous user traffic, we utilize the application purely as a “canary
in the coal mine”: its latency and error rates serve as high-level indicators of the underlying
platform instability (e.g., node exhaustion or network partition) that our agents aim to diagnose.
Complementing this setup, we integrate ChaosBlade [5] for infrastructure-level perturbations (e.g.,
network loss, disk pressure) and utilize atomic kubectl commands for configuration mutations,
ensuring the benchmark covers the full spectrum of cloud reliability challenges.
3.3.2
Phase 1: Fault Knowledge Base Construction. To comprehensively evaluate the diagnostic
capabilities of Agentic RCA, we collect a diverse fault dataset by synthesizing information from
multiple complementary sources. First, we extracted specification-based misconfigurations from
official Kubernetes documentation and deployment tutorials[19], which clarify deterministic re-
lationships between configuration parameters and potential failure consequences (e.g., incorrect
service selector resulting in traffic routing failures). Second, we extracted empirical operational faults
from developer communities (e.g., Stack Overflow)[22, 42], capturing errors frequently encountered
by users. Finally, we supplemented these with established academic paper [4, 7, 18] to ensure
coverage of widely recognized canonical failure modes in the AIOps community.
Raw data from these sources are typically unstructured and combine narrative prose with
fragmented code snippets. To transform this into a usable knowledge base, we leverage Gemini 3
, Vol. 1, No. 1, Article . Publication date: March 2026.


--- Page 8 ---
8
Yilun Wang, Guangba Yu et al.
Table 2. Comprehensive Taxonomy of Kubernetes Faults in Cloud-OpsBench. Difficulty levels (Easy/Medi-
um/Hard) correspond to the diagnostic reasoning complexity.

48

40 distinct fault types
-
452
Pro, a state-of-the-art LLM, to extract structured metadata via few-shot prompting. We define a
uniform fault reproduction model composed of two core dimensions:
• Semantic Metadata: This component includes the fault name, a symptom description, and the
root cause logic. These details provide the agent with natural language context to understand the
causality chain (e.g., Pod remains Pending because node taints block scheduling).
• Deployment Artifacts: To ensure deterministic execution, we formalize the reproduction
steps into a tuple ⟨P, A, S⟩.(i) Prerequisites (P) capture the necessary cluster state pre-injection
(e.g., ResourceQuotas); (ii) Fault Artifact (A) represents the core defective configuration (e.g., a
misconfigured YAML) or the active injection rule (e.g., a ChaosBlade command); (iii) Activation
Sequence (S) defines the ordered operations to apply P and A.
To ensure the accuracy and practical utility of the Fault Knowledge Base, we validate both the
semantic quality of fault descriptions and reproducibility of faults. First, we employ an LLM-as-a-
Judge[59] mechanism base on Gemini 3 Pro to iteratively score and refine the fault descriptions,
thereby enhancing their technical accuracy and readability for humans. These pre-filtered candidates
are then presented to cloud-native experts for the final review. As illustrated in Figure 2, experts
focus on validating the semantic logic of root causes and refining the technical details of the
deployment artifacts. Finally, to verify execution fidelity, we deploy the approved artifacts in a
sandbox cluster, confirming that the formalized model ⟨P, A, S⟩consistently triggers expected
symptoms. Only entries passing this triad of checks (LLM Judge →Human Expert →Runtime
Verification) are committed to the final Knowledge Base. Finally, we build a comprehensive fault
knowledge base organized around the rigorous taxonomy detailed in Table 2. Our taxonomy
encompasses 7 distinct fault categories spanning the full Kubernetes stack. Within these categories,
we define 40 specific root cause types, ensuring fine-grained coverage.
3.3.3
Phase 2: Automatic Fault Case Generation. Traditional fault injection relies heavily on manual
orchestration, which is labor-intensive and unscalable. To automate this, we employ a Multi-Agent
System (MAS) that mirrors a collaborative engineering team. As illustrated in Fig. 2, the system
, Vol. 1, No. 1, Article . Publication date: March 2026.


--- Page 9 ---
Cloud-OpsBench: A Reproducible Benchmark for Agentic Root Cause Analysis in Cloud Systems
9
orchestrates three agents in a closed loop to transform static fault knowledge into reproducible
benchmark cases.
1. Generation-Correction Loop. The core workflow operates as a closed-loop iterative process
designed to overcome the inherent resilience of cloud systems. The cycle initiates with Generator
Agent, acting as the architect. By synthesizing the structured fault knowledge with real-time cluster
info, it employs Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning to construct a precise fault reproduction triplet
M = ⟨P, A, S⟩, which encapsulates the prerequisites, fault artifact, and activation sequence.
This model is immediately operationalized by Executor Agent, which serves as the infrastructure
interface. It translates the abstract activation sequence S into imperative low-level operations (e.g.,
applying Kubernetes manifests via kubectl or executing chaos scripts) and strictly enforces the
deployment order to ensure the fault is physically injected into the live Kubernetes environment.
Following execution, Verifier Agent continuously monitors telemetry to perform a rigorous
validation check. Crucially, if the injection fails to trigger the expected anomaly, which is often
due to Kubernetes automatically masking the fault (e.g., rescheduling a Pod to bypass a taint),
Verifier Agent diagnoses the discrepancy between the intended logic and the actual runtime state.
It then instructs Generator Agent to refine the injection parameters (e.g., increasing resource stress
intensity). This self-correcting loop persists until the fault is reliably verified, ensuring that every
generated case is ecologically valid.
2. State Snapshot. Upon verification, the State Snapshot module materializes the paradigm pro-
posed in Section 3.2. The system freezes the crime scene by preserving raw telemetry (a fixed
window of metrics, logs, and objects). To construct the mocked operational interface, we perform
an exhaustive parameter sweep using our diagnostic toolset (T1–T10, Table 3). The module pre-
executes every plausible diagnostic command against the frozen data, effectively pre-rendering the
Digital Twin’s responses:
• Resource Enumeration: The system iterates through all standard Kubernetes objects (e.g., Pods,
Services, PVCs) in the target namespace. For every identified object, it executes retrieval tools
(T1, T2) to capture their full specifications, runtime statuses, and events.
• Service Topology & Telemetry: For each registered service, the system executes diagnostic
tools to freeze its application state. This includes: (i) running reachability checks (T6) against
target service ports; (ii) fetching static configurations (T3) and dependency graphs (T4); and (iii)
extracting and analyzing logs via both raw retrieval (T5) and error pattern summary (T9).
• Cluster-Wide Context: To capture the global context, the system scans all nodes to record
hardware capacity, metadata (T7) and cluster component health (T10), while processing all raw
metrics into structured anomaly alerts (T8).
The resulting JSON repository serves as the immutable persistence layer. To ensure the snapshot
effectively covers the investigative search space, we enforce a Maximum Information Coverage
strategy. On average, each fault case generates 487 distinct tool invocations. This exhaustive pre-
computation guarantees that valid agent queries (e.g., correct service names and ports) will hit
a deterministic entry, while invalid queries (e.g., non-existent pods) map to realistic Not Found
errors, thereby faithfully simulating the boundaries of a live system.
Crucially, this finite set of pre-computed states does not trivialize the diagnostic challenge. We
design the interaction paradigm to enforce combinatorial complexity rather than atomic selection.
First, the agent operates in an open-ended generation setting. Since the mock interface simulates an
infinite parameter space by returning realistic errors for invalid inputs, the agent must syntactically
generate precise commands rather than selecting from a closed menu. Second, a successful diagnosis
requires a coherent chain of interdependent steps (typically 𝑁≈10), creating a reasoning search
, Vol. 1, No. 1, Article . Publication date: March 2026.


--- Page 10 ---
10
Yilun Wang, Guangba Yu et al.
Table 3. Description of specialized diagnostic tools in Cloud-OpsBench
Category
Tool Name
Arguments
Output Description
Resource
Inspection
T1.GetResources
Args: resource_type, namespace, resource_name
List resources in a namespace with status and extended attributes.
T2.DescribeResource
Args: resource_type, resource_name, namespace
Get runtime details of a specific resource: state, conditions, and events.
T3.GetAppYAML
Args: service_name
Get the deployment configuration YAML for a given service.
Service
Interaction
T4.GetServiceDependencies
Args: service_name
Get service dependencies in a tree structure.
T6.CheckServiceConnectivity
Args: namespace, service_name, port
Test service reachability via TCP handshake, returns connection success/failure.
Telemetry
Analysis
T8.GetAlerts
Args: -
Get alerts for cluster metric anomalies generated by a threshold-based detector.
Returns abnormal metrics with deviation magnitude.
T5.GetRecentLogs
Args: service_name, namespace
Get recent logs of a service in a namespace for error detection (default: 50 lines).
T9.GetErrorLogs
Args: service_name, namespace
Return a characteristic summary of abnormal logs by matching error keywords
(e.g., ERROR, FAIL).
Infra.
Diagnostics
T7.GetClusterConfiguration
Args: -
Get cluster-wide node details such as resources, labels, taints, and status.
T10.CheckNodeServiceStatus
Args: node_name, component_name
Probes liveness of control plane components on a node. Returns process status,
runtime state, and recent log snippets.
space of 487𝑁. This combinatorial explosion ensures that random guessing is statistically impossible,
thereby maintaining the benchmark’s rigor as a test of systematic deductive logic.
3. Ground Truth Annotation. To evaluate both the final diagnosis and the investigative process,
the module automatically synthesizes a ground truth tuple G∗= ⟨R∗, T ∗⟩. The first component,
the true root cause (R∗), is derived directly from the Generator Agent’s design intent, specifying
the exact location (e.g., node-1) and fault category to serve as the gold standard for evaluating
diagnosis accuracy.
To support process-oriented evaluation, we synthesize a canonical diagnostic path by inverting
the injection logic. Since Generator Agent possesses omniscient knowledge of the fault artifact
A, we employ a rule-based engine to deterministically map the injected fault (e.g., “Added a
Taint”) to the specific verification step required to reveal it (e.g., “Execute describe node tool”).
This inversion process yields a critical evidence chain that serves as the logical skeleton for the
ground truth, structured around two design principles: (i) Strict Causal Precedence (Anti-Guessing):
We define the trajectory not merely as a set of actions, but as a directed graph where symptom
discovery (identifying the observable anomaly) is a logical prerequisite for root cause verification
(confirming the defect). This structure ensures that the ground truth captures the full deductive
chain, distinguishing valid reasoning from unfounded shortcuts. (ii) Exploratory Noise Tolerance (Pro-
Redundancy): Recognizing that realistic troubleshooting involves trial-and-error, we define T ∗as a
minimal mandatory subsequence rather than a rigid script. This definition inherently accommodates
interstitial heuristic actions (e.g., checking logs that turn out to be empty), ensuring that the ground
truth represents the necessity of evidence without excluding the reality of exploration.
4
Evaluation
In this section, we evaluate LLM agents on Cloud-OpsBench to answer the following RQs:
• RQ1 (Outcome Effectiveness): How accurately can LLM agents diagnose root causes?
• RQ2 (Process Alignment): Do agents follow human expert diagnostic paths?
• RQ3 (Tool Robustness): How robust are LLM agents in invoking diagnostic tools?
• RQ4 (Knowledge Efficacy): Does external knowledge improve diagnostic performance?
, Vol. 1, No. 1, Article . Publication date: March 2026.


--- Page 11 ---
Cloud-OpsBench: A Reproducible Benchmark for Agentic Root Cause Analysis in Cloud Systems
11
4.1
Experimental Setup
The evaluating agent framework is implemented in Python 3.10, utilizing CrewAI[9] as the orches-
tration engine and Pydantic[38] for structured tool interface definitions. Consistent with white-box
design principles, we employ Langfuse[23] for comprehensive agent observability, which automati-
cally captures detailed traces of LLM generations, reasoning steps, and tool invocations during the
diagnostic process.
We evaluate the benchmark using seven models, explicitly categorized into two tiers to assess
the impact of parameter scale. The Large Language Models tier comprises high-capacity frontier
models, including proprietary leaders (GPT-5, GPT-4o, Claude-4-Sonnet) and open-source giants
(DeepSeek-V3.2, Qwen3-235B). In contrast, the Small Language Models (SLMs) tier [60] represents
efficiency-optimized baselines with parameters under 20B, specifically Qwen3-14B and Qwen3-8B.
4.2
Evaluation Metrics
To provide a holistic assessment, we evaluate performance across two dimensions: Outcome Effec-
tiveness and Process Quality. Unlike prior works [7, 18, 36, 49, 53] that only rely on outcome-based
metrics which inadvertently reward lucky guesses, our framework quantifies the logical fidelity of
the diagnosis. By aligning the agent’s trajectory with expert-annotated paths (§ 3.3.3), we distinguish
systematic reasoning from stochastic trial-and-error.
4.2.1
Outcome-based Metrics. These metrics evaluate the final diagnostic conclusion. A diagnosis
is considered correct only if it strictly matches the ground truth R∗in three dimensions: fault
lifecycle stage, faulty component, and failure reason.
• Top-k Accuracy Rate (𝐴@𝑘)[36, 51, 53]: This measures the probability that the correct root
cause is included in agent’s top-𝑘predictions. Let 𝑟𝑐𝑖denote the set of ground truth for the 𝑖-th
case, and 𝐸𝑘
𝑖be the top-𝑘predicted elements. The metric is defined as: 𝐴@𝑘= 1
|𝐼|
Í|𝐼|
𝑖=1 I(𝑟𝑐𝑖⊆𝐸𝑘
𝑖).
We evaluate with 𝑘= 1, 3. A higher 𝐴@𝑘indicates better accuracy in identifying root causes.
• Task Completion Rate (TCR): Proxies the agent’s operational stability and instruction-following
capability. It is defined as the ratio of episodes where the agent successfully outputs a structured
diagnosis without crashing or violating the output schema.
4.2.2
Process-based Metrics. To quantify reasoning quality, we compare the agent’s tool execution
trajectory 𝑇𝑎𝑔𝑒𝑛𝑡= [𝑎1, . . . ,𝑎𝑛] against the expert ground truth path 𝑇𝑔𝑜𝑙𝑑= [𝑔1, . . . ,𝑔𝑚]. An action
𝑎𝑖matches 𝑔𝑗if and only if both the tool name and critical arguments are identical. Let S𝑇be the
set of distinct tool calls in 𝑇.
• Trajectory Alignment. We employ three strictness levels to evaluate how closely the agent
mimics expert procedures [13, 20]: (1) Exact Match measures how exactly the agent replicate the
expert’s sequence of tool invocations, with no deviations in action or order. This stringent metric
reflects a scenario where only the canonical diagnostic path is considered correct. (2) Any-Order
Match: measures functional completeness by checking whether all necessary diagnostic actions
in the gold trajectory are present in the agent’s execution, regardless of order or extra steps.
(3) In-Order Match measures whether the agent performs all essential diagnostic steps in the
correct relative order, while allowing arbitrary extra actions.
• Tool Usage Efficiency. To assess the quality of tool selection independent of execution order,
we measure Tool Relevance and Tool Coverage [46]. Relevance (𝑅𝑒𝑙𝑒𝑣𝑎𝑛𝑐𝑒=
|S𝑎𝑔𝑒𝑛𝑡∩S𝑔𝑜𝑙𝑑|
|S𝑎𝑔𝑒𝑛𝑡|
)
quantifies the fraction of the agent’s tool invocations that align with expert practice, while
Coverage (𝐶𝑜𝑣𝑒𝑟𝑎𝑔𝑒=
|S𝑎𝑔𝑒𝑛𝑡∩S𝑔𝑜𝑙𝑑|
|S𝑔𝑜𝑙𝑑|
) measures the proportion of essential diagnostic tools that
the agent successfully employed. To assess operational overhead, we measure diagnostic efficiency
, Vol. 1, No. 1, Article . Publication date: March 2026.


--- Page 12 ---
12

86.53
0.16
0.27
via Mean Time to Identify (MTTI) and Steps (total tool invocations). Lower values indicate faster
and more concise troubleshooting.
• Operational Robustness. We define three penalty metrics to quantify distinct failure modes:
(1) Invalid Action Count (IAC) counts the number of tool invocations in a diagnostic episode
that fail due to malformed arguments, hallucinated parameters, or violations of the expected
tool schema. (2) Redundant Action Rate (RAR) is the fraction of redundant actions without
new context or state change, indicating either reasoning stagnation or excessive re-confirmation.
(3) Zero-Tool Diagnosis Rate (ZTDR) quantifies speculative hallucination by measuring the
fraction of episodes where the agent directly generates a final result without performing any
valid tool invocations, indicating reliance on parametric knowledge over evidence gathering.
4.3
RQ1: RCA Outcome Effectiveness
We present the evaluation results in Tab. 4 and Fig. 3. Tab. 4 provides a comparative analysis of
seven LLM agents across outcome effectiveness and process quality metrics. Complementing this,
Fig. 3 visualizes the performance distribution across seven specific fault scenarios.
High accuracy necessitates extensive exploration rather than rushed convergence. Our
results indicate a positive correlation between diagnostic accuracy and the depth of exploration,
challenging the assumption that shorter diagnosis implies higher efficiency. DeepSeek-V3.2 achieves
the highest 𝐴@1 (0.73) alongside the highest average step count (10.0) and Coverage (0.88). This
pattern reflects an exhaustive verification strategy where the model systematically validates metrics,
logs, and events. Conversely, GPT-4o demonstrates premature convergence. It records the lowest
MTTI (23.27s) and shorter trajectories (5.67 steps) but yields significantly lower accuracy (𝐴@1 =
0.49). This divergence suggests that current agents lack an optimized termination mechanism
calibrated for accuracy. Models employing redundant verification successfully identify complex
root causes, whereas models prioritizing rapid conclusion often overlook critical evidence located
deep within the system state.
Syntactic validity constitutes a primary bottleneck for SLMs. While reasoning capability
is often considered the primary differentiator, our analysis reveals that SLM performance is con-
strained by fundamental tool-use correctness. Although the Task Completion Rate (TCR) remains
high across all models (>0.82), the Invalid Action Count (IAC) metric exposes a significant disparity.
SLMs such as Qwen3-14B exhibit an IAC of 0.40, which is approximately 10× higher than that of
GPT-5 (0.04). Trace analysis confirms that these models frequently generate invalid Kubernetes
API payloads, such as referencing non-existent Pod names or producing malformed JSON. Unlike
code generation tasks where compiler feedback is precise, runtime API errors are often ambiguous.
Consequently, syntactic robustness functions as a strict prerequisite. An agent must possess the
capability to generate valid queries to retrieve the telemetry required for subsequent reasoning.
, Vol. 1, No. 1, Article . Publication date: March 2026.


--- Page 13 ---
Cloud-OpsBench: A Reproducible Benchmark for Agentic Root Cause Analysis in Cloud Systems
13
Performance stratification is determined by symptom explicitness and observability.
Diagnostic effectiveness varies significantly based on the logical distance between the symptom and
the root cause. As visualized in the radar charts of Fig. 3, model performance follows a consistent
trend across fault categories:
• Explicit Faults: In Startup and Runtime scenarios, agents achieve high accuracy (Avg 𝐴@1 >
0.65). These faults produce explicit signals in Kubernetes Events (e.g., CrashLoopBackOff or
OOMKilled). The diagnosis primarily involves symptom matching, which requires mapping a
visible error code to a known cause.
• Implicit Faults: Performance declines in Admission and Performance scenarios (Avg 𝐴@1 < 0.36).
In these cases, the root cause location (e.g., resource contention) is decoupled from the symptom
manifestation. Resolution requires cross-layer causal inference to correlate application latency
with underlying infrastructure metrics.
This stratification indicates that current agents are proficient at processing explicit telemetry
logs but face challenges in establishing holistic system topology representations when errors are
distributed or lack direct error signals.
4.4
RQ2: RCA Process Alignment
To determine whether agents mimic human experts or develop independent reasoning patterns,
we analyze the alignment metrics in Table 4. The data reveals a significant divergence between
RCA result and procedural mimicry.
Functional completeness consistently supersedes procedural linearity. Across all models,
Any-Order Match scores consistently outperform In-Order Match scores (e.g., for Qwen3-235B, 0.41
vs. 0.38). This gap quantifies the exploration overhead. Agents typically perform the necessary
verification steps required by the ground truth but often intersperse them with irrelevant or
redundant actions. The substantial drop from Any-Order to In-Order alignment highlights that
while agents are proficient at identifying what information is needed (Information Retrieval),
they struggle to organize these actions into the logical, linear deductive chain (Logical Planning)
characteristic of human experts.
Operational redundancy serves as a necessary mechanism for reliability. Conventional
wisdom in software engineering prioritizes execution efficiency (minimizing steps). However, our
data suggests a redundancy paradox in agentic reasoning. Comparing the Redundant Action Rate
(RAR), the highest-performing model, DeepSeek-V3.2, exhibits a notable RAR of 0.11, indicating
that 11% of its actions are redundant. As illustrated in Case #1 of Fig. 4, where DeepSeek diagnoses
an Admission failure, the agent intersperses redundant verification steps (yellow nodes) with
progressive diagnostic actions to confirm system states before isolating the root cause. In contrast,
the underperforming GPT-4o shows a near-zero RAR of 0.02. This correlation implies that what
appears as inefficiency is functionally cognitive self-correction. High-performing agents utilize
redundant checks to confirm transient system states or validate hypotheses before committing to a
conclusion. Conversely, agents that strictly optimize for non-redundant paths often suffer from
fragile reasoning, lacking the verification loops necessary to filter out hallucinations.
Top-tier models exhibit divergent reasoning strategies. While both DeepSeek-V3.2 and GPT-
5 achieve SOTA accuracy, their underlying process metrics reveal fundamentally different reasoning
paradigms: (i) Coverage-Centric Strategy (DeepSeek-V3.2): This model adopts a brute-force
approach, maximizing evidence collection. It records the highest Steps (10.0) and Coverage (0.88),
ensuring that nearly all expert-defined critical information is retrieved. This strategy prioritizes
recall over precision, accepting higher redundancy (RAR=0.11) to guarantee diagnostic safety. (2)
Relevance-Centric Strategy (GPT-5): Conversely, GPT-5 mimics the efficiency of a seasoned
, Vol. 1, No. 1, Article . Publication date: March 2026.


--- Page 14 ---
14
Yilun Wang, Guangba Yu et al.
A@1                                                              A@3                                                               TCR
In-Order                                                    Any-Order                                                     Relevance 
Coverage                                                          Steps                                                      
MTTI 
IAC                                                                  RAR                                                     
ZTDR
Fig. 3. Details of metrics across 7 fault categories.
expert. It achieves the highest Relevance (0.65) with significantly fewer steps (5.57). This indicates
a high signal-to-noise ratio in tool selection, where the model filters out exploratory noise and
targets critical system states directly. This divergence implies that there is a trade-off between the
safety of exhaustive search and the efficiency of heuristic leaps.
Tool selection precision dissociates from diagnostic success in SLMs. Our analysis uncovers
a competence illusion in SLMs. As shown in Table 4, Qwen3-14B achieves a Tool Relevance score
of 0.63, matching that of the much larger GPT-4o (0.63) and nearly approaching GPT-5 (0.65). This
indicates that the model possesses strong semantic knowledge regarding which tools are appropriate
for a given context (Classifier Competence). However, this high relevance does not translate to
outcome effectiveness (𝐴@1 = 0.34 vs. GPT-5’s 0.67). This gap suggests that the bottleneck for
SLMs is not tool selection (identifying the right API) but information integration (interpreting the
, Vol. 1, No. 1, Article . Publication date: March 2026.


--- Page 15 ---
Cloud-OpsBench: A Reproducible Benchmark for Agentic Root Cause Analysis in Cloud Systems
15
---------- Correct Output Format ----------
Thought: I will retrieve the cluster configuration.
Action: GetClusterConfiguration
Action Input: {}
------------- Agent Output -------------
Thought: I will retrieve the cluster configuration.
Action: GetClusterConfiguration
# Parsing Error: Do not provide arguments
Query: Service quality degradation
------------- Agent Output -------------
(Step 1)
Final Answer: Alerts show frontend experiencing 
critical HighLatency…                    # Hallucination
Case #2
Case #4
🟢GetResources {pods}
à All pods runing
🟢GetResources {deployments}  à frontend 0/1 ready
🟡GetResources {pods, app=frontend}
🟡GetResources {pods, output_wide}
🟢DescribeResource {deployments, frontend}
🟢└─ DescribeResource {replicaset, frontend} 
à forbidden: exceeded quota
🔴GetResources {resourcequota}  à requests.cpu: 10/10 
🟡GetResources {pods, show_labels}
🟡DescribeResource {resourcequotas}
🟡GetAppYAML {frontend}
Final Answer: ‘resourcequota exceed’
---------- Agent Output ----------
…
(Step i)
Thought: List pod where app=adservice
Action: GetResources
Action Input: {’pod’,’app=adservice’}
Output: null
(Step i+1)
Thought: List pod where app=adservice
Action: GetResources
Action Input: {’pod’,’app=adservice’}
# Repetition Loop
Output: null
Case #3
🟢Progress
🟡Redundant
🔴Root Cause
Case #1
step
Fig. 4. Qualitative case studies illustrating distinct diagnostic behaviors and failure modes.
API response). While they can correctly identify the need to check logs, they fail to synthesize the
returned log data into a causal chain, highlighting that tool selection and diagnostic reasoning are
distinct capability layers that scale differently with model size.
4.5
RQ3: Tool Robustness
Robustness evaluates the agent’s ability to generate valid tool invocations and recover from runtime
execution errors. We analyze the Invalid Action Count (IAC) and Zero-Tool Diagnosis Rate (ZTDR)
alongside the qualitative behaviors visualized in Fig. 4.
Lack of self-correction capabilities leads to reasoning stagnation in SLMs. The critical
differentiator between model tiers is not merely the occurrence of errors, but the ability to uti-
lize runtime feedback for recovery. As illustrated in Case#2 of Fig. 4, when small models (e.g.,
Qwen3-14B) encounter an API error (e.g., Pod not found), they fail to interpret the error message
as corrective feedback. Instead of revising the query, they enter a repetition loop, retrying the
exact same invalid payload until the step limit is exhausted. In contrast, large-scale models like
DeepSeek-V3.2 demonstrate reflective debugging, where they analyze the error trace and suc-
cessfully reformulate the tool invocation in the subsequent step. This indicates that robustness is
defined by the meta-cognitive loop of error handling, which is largely absent in sub-20B models.
Proprietary models exhibit laziness via speculative shortcuts. Robustness also encompasses
the discipline to strictly adhere to evidence-based workflows. We identify a premature conclusion
failure mode in Claude-4-Sonnet, quantifiable by its high ZTDR of 0.32. As depicted in Case#4 of
Fig. 4, this model occasionally bypasses the investigation phase entirely, hallucinating a specific root
cause (e.g., claiming “High Latency due to CPU”) based solely on the generic alert description. While
this behavior reduces steps (Avg 4.25), it compromises reliability (𝐴@1 = 0.5). True robustness
requires the agent to suppress the parametric urge to generate a probable answer and instead verify
the system state through execution, ensuring that every diagnosis is backed by runtime telemetry.
Schema Hallucination undermines tool utility. Beyond syntax errors, we observe a semantic
failure mode in mid-tier models termed schema hallucination (Case #3 in Fig. 4). Agents frequently
invent non-existent parameters for valid tools (e.g., passing filters to parameter-less APIs). Unlike
simple syntax errors, these hallucinations suggest a fundamental misalignment between the agent’s
internal representation of the tool and the actual API definition. This decoupling prevents the agent
from utilizing the toolset effectively, even when the reasoning intent is correct.
4.6
RQ4: Knowledge Efficacy
In this section, we evaluate three mainstream knowledge enhancement strategies against the zero-
shot baseline: (i) Chain-of-Thought (CoT), which enforces a structured reasoning template (e.g.,
Status →Events →Config →Cluster) to guide pre-action planning. (ii) In-Context Learning (ICL),
which retrieves 3 historical diagnostic traces from the same fault category, including tool invocations
, Vol. 1, No. 1, Article . Publication date: March 2026.


--- Page 16 ---
16
Yilun Wang, Guangba Yu et al.

0
and outcomes as dynamic demonstrations. (iii) Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), which
injects relevant knowledge from authoritative Kubernetes operations documentation [24] to ground
domain understanding. The results in Table 5 indicate that providing procedural demonstrations is
more effective than providing declarative knowledge or encouraging abstract reasoning.
Procedural demonstration (ICL) outweighs declarative knowledge retrieval (RAG). A
consistent trend across all models is the dominance of In-Context Learning (ICL). For GPT-4o, ICL
improves A@1 from 0.49 to 0.7, significantly outperforming RAG (0.61). This performance gap
illuminates the nature of the agentic bottleneck. RAG injects authoritative Kubernetes operations
documentation, which helps agents understand what a tool does (enhancing Coverage), but fails
to instruct how to orchestrate these tools in a multi-step diagnosis. In contrast, ICL retrieves
historical diagnostic traces, providing concrete examples of valid tool invocations and outcome
interpretations. This suggests that agents benefit more from mimicking the execution patterns of
experts (Procedural Norms) than from reading the manuals of the system (Declarative Knowledge).
Few-shot demonstrations effectively bridge the scale gap for SLMs. One of the most
profound findings is the disproportionate benefit ICL confers on SLMs. Under the Base setting,
Qwen3-14B (A@1=0.34) lags far behind GPT-4o (0.49). However, with ICL, Qwen3-14B achieves a
A@1 of 0.71, effectively matching or even surpassing the performance of the unprompted GPT-5 tier.
Table 5 reveals the mechanism behind this resurrection: ICL significantly reduces the Invalid Action
Count (IAC) for Qwen3-14B from 0.40 to 0.29. This indicates that SLMs possess latent reasoning
capabilities that are suppressed by syntactic fragility. By observing valid tool invocation examples
in the context window, SLMs can copy-paste the correct schema structure, bypassing the syntax
barrier identified in RQ3 and unlocking their reasoning potential.
Structured reasoning templates (CoT) yield diminishing returns. Contrary to its success in
logical reasoning tasks, the CoT strategy, which enforces a “Status →Events →Config” template,
proves ineffective for Agentic RCA. For Qwen3-235B, CoT degrades performance (0.50 →0.47), and
for GPT-4o, it offers no gain. We attribute this to template rigidity and hallucination amplification.
First, a fixed pre-action planning template may not align with the dynamic nature of diverse
faults (e.g., a network issue requires a different flow than a crash loop). Second, without external
grounding (like RAG or ICL), forcing a model to generate a long reasoning chain often induces it
to hallucinate plausible but incorrect intermediate states. This indicates that reasoning structure
alone is insufficient without the grounding of valid execution history or external knowledge.
, Vol. 1, No. 1, Article . Publication date: March 2026.


--- Page 17 ---
Cloud-OpsBench: A Reproducible Benchmark for Agentic Root Cause Analysis in Cloud Systems
17
5
Discussion
5.1
Significance and Impact
Redefining the Evaluation Standard for Autonomous SRE. Prior to Cloud-OpsBench, the field
relied on outcome-based metrics that inadvertently rewarded stochastic guessing [2, 3, 36, 49, 53]
. This work invalidates that approach. By enforcing a process-centric evaluation paradigm, we
demonstrate that how an agent verifies is as critical as what it concludes. We establish the first
rigorous standard for differentiating between fragile probability generation and robust systematic
reasoning in industrial reliability tasks.
Diagnosing the Diagnosers. Beyond aggregate performance rankings, this work provides a
rigorous taxonomy of failure mechanisms in Agentic RCA. We empirically isolate distinct cognitive
deficits: Syntactic Fragility in SLMs and Parametric Hallucination in frontier models. This white-box
analysis suggests that future improvements should focus on targeted interventions (e.g., syntax-
constrained decoding or verification loops) rather than relying solely on parameter scaling.
Democratizing Research via Zero-Cost Replay. Prior to Cloud-OpsBench, the high barrier of
entry which requires expensive cloud clusters and incurring lengthy feedback loops (often hours
per run [7]) effectively gated Agentic RCA research to industrial labs with abundant resources. By
decoupling system state from runtime execution, we reduce the evaluation cost from substantial
cloud bills to negligible computation. Cloud-OpsBench enables a full-scale benchmark run in
seconds on a standard laptop. This infrastructure empowers the broader academic community to
iterate rapidly on agent designs without the friction of deployment overhead.
5.2
Future Research Opportunities
Cloud-OpsBench serves as an extensible environment for next-generation SRE research. We map
our empirical insights to specific future research opportunities.
From Monolithic to Multi-Agent Architectures. Our identification of a competence mis-
match where SLMs fail due to rigid schema compliance despite having sufficient semantic intuition
proves that generic reasoning capability is not the sole bottleneck. To address this, future re-
search can abandon the one-model-fits-all approach in favor of multi-agent architectures based on
Cloud-OpsBench. For example, researchers can explore multi-agent architectures where roles are
specialized: a planner agent (powered by robust models like GPT-5) orchestrates the diagnostic
logic, while lightweight executor agents (fine-tuned SLMs) handle specific tool interactions, thereby
optimizing the cost-accuracy trade-off.
From Open-Ended Prompting to RL Policies. The reasoning stagnation and premature con-
vergence observed in RQ2 (§ 4.4) indicate that current agents operate as open-loop systems lacking
an optimal stopping mechanism. By functioning as a high-fidelity Digital Twin, Cloud-OpsBench
transforms AIOps into a safe reinforcement learning environment. We encourage researchers to
utilize process metrics (Steps, RAR) as reward signals to train agents via PPO [40] or DPO [39].
This shifts the paradigm from static prompt engineering to dynamic policy optimization, allowing
agents to learn exploration strategies that balance thoroughness with efficiency.
From Static Manuals to Self-Evolving Data Engines. Our RQ4 findings (§ 4.6) demonstrate
that procedural demonstrations (ICL) significantly outperform declarative documentation (RAG),
suggesting that SRE knowledge is best represented by trajectories rather than static manuals. This
establishes Cloud-OpsBench as a data engine capable of harvesting massive expert-level diagnostic
traces. This infrastructure unlocks critical avenues for Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) [34] to align
small models with expert behaviors and drives automated prompt optimization (APO) [64], where
diagnostic outcomes serve as feedback to scientifically evolve agent instructions (e.g., via DSPy [21])
without expensive retraining.
, Vol. 1, No. 1, Article . Publication date: March 2026.


--- Page 18 ---
18
Yilun Wang, Guangba Yu et al.
5.3
Threats to Validity
External Validity. While Cloud-OpsBench covers 7 diverse fault categories, it is grounded in
a Kubernetes microservices environment. The specific toolsets may not generalize perfectly to
monolithic architectures or serverless platforms, though the fundamental reasoning patterns
(resource contention, cascading failure) remain transferable.
Internal Validity. The prompt engineering strategies (CoT, ICL) were implemented using generic
templates to ensure fairness. It is possible that model-specific prompt optimization could yield
different performance ceilings. However, our goal was to benchmark the foundational capabilities
of the models themselves rather than the efficacy of prompt tuning.
Construct Validity. A potential concern regarding the State Snapshot paradigm is that it restricts
the agent’s ability to observe real-time state transitions, effectively freezing the crime scene. We
acknowledge this limitation but emphasize that for RCA, the “post-mortem” context, compris-
ing Kubernetes object status, time-series metrics and timestamped logs, inherently captures the
vast majority (typically > 90%) of diagnostic clues required for logical deduction [1]. Therefore,
Cloud-OpsBench is rigorously scoped to evaluate reasoning based on existing evidence, explicitly
distinguishing itself from downstream tasks that require trial-and-error remediation or active
environmental intervention.
6
Related Work
Evolution of RCA. Traditional RCA methods primarily formulated diagnosis as a discriminative
task, utilizing causal graphs or neural networks to identify anomalies [16, 25, 26, 29, 30, 51–53, 62].
The integration of LLMs later shifted the paradigm towards generative diagnosis, leveraging RAG
to retrieve expert knowledge for passive analysis [1, 8, 11, 28, 47]. However, most LLM-based
methods remain passive, analyzing only provided snapshots without the ability to interactively
verify hypotheses.
To enable active diagnosis, recent research has shifted toward agentic workflows [14]. Unlike
static LLM-based approaches, agents are designed to actively reason and interact with complex
environments via code execution or API calls. This trend has rapidly permeated various layers of the
technology stack, ranging from diagnosing distributed cloud and AI workloads to resolving database
failures via expert-aligned reasoning [33, 35, 44, 45, 50, 61]. These advancements collectively signal
a consensus that agentic workflows capable of multi-step reasoning and tool utilization are essential
for addressing the non-deterministic nature of modern software failures.
Benchmarking in AIOps. Current benchmarks face a fundamental trade-off between repro-
ducibility and ecological validity, which limits the scope of evaluation metrics. Static artifact
benchmarks [2, 3, 10, 25, 36, 41, 53, 57, 65] rely on offline datasets collected from historical inci-
dents. While offering high determinism, they inherently lack interactivity. Recent works [49] have
advanced this paradigm by allowing agents to query data. However, this interaction is restricted to
generative tooling, which requires agents to synthesize Python code to parse local CSV files. While
this tests data manipulation skills, it fails to capture the standard tool-use central to real-world
SRE operations. Furthermore, evaluation in this paradigm is strictly outcome-based (e.g., accuracy),
ignoring whether the agent arrived at the root cause through valid reasoning.
Conversely, dynamic benchmarks [7, 18, 43] prioritize ecological validity by deploying live
microservice applications. Although these environments support standard tool use, they suffer
from inherent stochasticity. Environmental noise, such as network jitter or race conditions, renders
experiments non-deterministic, making it difficult to disentangle agent reasoning errors from
system instability. Consequently, these benchmarks are also forced to rely on outcome-based
, Vol. 1, No. 1, Article . Publication date: March 2026.


--- Page 19 ---
Cloud-OpsBench: A Reproducible Benchmark for Agentic Root Cause Analysis in Cloud Systems
19
metrics, as the non-deterministic environment precludes precise alignment and scoring of the
agent’s investigative process.
As summarized in Table 1, Cloud-OpsBench bridges this gap by introducing the State Snapshot
paradigm. By simulating standard tool-use via deterministic mocked interfaces, we achieve the high
ecological validity of dynamic environments while retaining the rigorous reproducibility of static
datasets. This unique combination enables not just outcome assessment, but also process-oriented
evaluation, measuring how well the agent’s actions align with expert SRE trajectories.
7
Conclusion
This paper introduces Cloud-OpsBench, the first large-scale benchmark that resolves the tension
between ecological validity and reproducibility in Agentic RCA via a deterministic state snapshot
paradigm. By constructing a noise-free Digital Twin of the cloud, we enable the rigorous process-
centric evaluation of SRE agents across 452 diverse fault cases. Our empirical analysis challenges the
efficiency-first orthodoxy, revealing that operational redundancy is essential for reliability and that
syntactic rigidity, not logic, bottlenecks SLMs. Ultimately, Cloud-OpsBench serves as an enabling
infrastructure for next-generation research, functioning as both a data engine for bootstrapping
SFT and a safe RL Sandbox for policy optimization, thereby laying the groundwork for evolving
agents from fragile chat-bots into robust self-improving reliability engineers.
8
Data Availability
We have made our collected dataset of 452 cloud fault cases and the code for running the experiments
publicly available at https://github.com/LLM4Ops/Cloud-OpsBench.
